memos.markdown:
# The Chip Reset and the $19 Billion Compute Lease

Semiconductors just had their worst month since 2008, and the market spent this week deciding whether that was a warning or a sale. The tell was at the top: **Apple reclaimed the world's-largest-company title from Nvidia** for the first time since April 2025, its "wait-and-see" AI posture suddenly re-rated as discipline rather than delay. Beneath the equity noise, the real story was contractual — **Anthropic's reported $19 billion compute arrangement with TeraWulf**, with a $3.5B upfront slug, priced AI infrastructure demand more concretely than any analyst note could. The reset is in the multiples, not the demand.

## Deal activity

The week was a pile of small tuck-ins wrapped around one very large question mark. Nine strategic M&A deals closed with no disclosed values — enterprise software roll-ups (**PartnerOne / ISI Analytics**), workforce tech (ORTEC absorbing a Belgian provider serving 225,000 frontline workers), and a smattering of fintech and IoT bolt-ons. When five closed deals in a row carry no price tag, the pattern is the message: this is the sub-$100M vertical-software grind, not a market clearing big risk.

The tonnage lived in the pending column. **Anthropic / TeraWulf at a reported $19B** dwarfs everything else and doubles as a financing stress test — the scrutiny is squarely on whether that $3.5B upfront covers what the buildout requires. Below it, **Micron committed $500M** to secure Texas wafer supply, and Korea's Nextflex sale (~**$580M**) slipped as its final-bid process stalled with no buyer in hand. Two regulatory clouds worth noting: state AGs filed an antitrust challenge to **Skydance / Paramount**, and Apple's unconfirmed grab of AI-infrastructure shop SigScalr suggests even the "wait-and-see" company is quietly buying capability.

## Capital markets

Credit is doing the heavy lifting while equity sulks. **NBIS landed a $775M debt deal** to fund its AI buildout — Nvidia-backed, and the market rewarded it with a rally, confirming private credit's appetite for data-center paper remains intact. Asset-based finance echoed the theme: **AIP Capital and Monroe Capital closed a $643M inaugural aircraft ABS**, a reminder that the securitization bid extends well beyond compute. CreditSights' mid-year hyperscaler issuance update frames the backdrop — capex trajectories that increasingly require the debt markets to absorb them.

Equity issuance was thinner and softer. **Zepto's IPO valuation is reportedly being cut sharply** ahead of listing, the clearest read yet on cooling emerging-market tech appetite, while Advasa Holdings filed an S-1/A amendment without disclosing proceeds. The message from the primary market: debt windows are open, equity windows want a discount.

## What we're watching

First, the earnings gate. **Alphabet and Tesla headline next week**, and Alphabet's tape is already jumpy — a 4% Friday drop against a bull thesis resting on cloud and ad growth, with Gemini delays as the live bear signal. Google reportedly running out of AI capacity means the capex number matters as much as the revenue line.

Second, the TeraWulf financing. If **Anthropic's $19B** structure gets refinanced or resized, it recalibrates how every AI compute lease gets underwritten. Watch the upfront-payment question resolve.

Third, the Skydance-Paramount antitrust timeline. The AG challenge introduces genuine closing risk into a deal the market had largely penciled in.

The bottom line: chips got cheaper this week, but compute got more expensive to finance — and that gap is where the next quarter of deals will be made.

# SK Hynix Crosses the Pacific: A $26.5B Listing Reprices Korean Memory

The week's defining event was SK Hynix's $26.5 billion Nasdaq listing—the largest offering ever by a Korean semiconductor firm—which arrived alongside a demand backdrop that made the timing look deliberate. Korea's first-decade July exports hit a record $29.8B on a 193% year-over-year surge in semiconductor shipments ($11.2B), and SK Hynix's US-listed market cap overtook Micron's on the strength of its HBM franchise. Yet the equity reaction was tellingly mixed: shares fell 3.7–4.4% in Seoul even as the ADR debuted, the won weakened toward 1,500 on the dollar supply the listing created, and Korea Investment flagged that Q2 profit will likely miss consensus. The cycle debate—Bank of Korea dismissing "peak" fears against the "AI supercycle" bulls—is now being adjudicated in real capital flows.

## Deal activity

M&A skewed decisively toward AI infrastructure and the semiconductor supply chain rather than horizontal consolidation. The largest disclosed transaction was Element Solutions' $14.5B acquisition of Solutics Advanced Materials, a pure play on semiconductor materials that mirrors Micron's smaller $500M strategic investment in wafer supplier GlobalWafers—two moves to lock down inputs ahead of a supply cliff.

The AI-power thesis produced its own cluster: TeraWulf's reported $19B agreement with Anthropic (structured with a $3.5B upfront payment that has drawn financing scrutiny), EQT's acquisition of Copia Power, Nvidia's reported minority interest in Stargate-linked Lancium, and Meta's undisclosed $6.5B cloud/AI capacity push. Compute and the electricity to run it are now the same deal.

Government-adjacent semiconductor intervention also surfaced, with reports the US is taking a 10% stake in Intel. Closer to home, Nextflex's ~$580M sale process stalled when the final-bid round was delayed with no buyers named—a reminder that mid-cap component assets still face financing and price gaps even in a hot sector. Outside tech, e& is exiting its Vodafone stake for $5.95B.

## Capital markets

The primary market centered on SK Hynix, which filed a 424B4 prospectus supplement with the SEC on July 10 ahead of its closed $26.5B listing—a rare instance of a Korean chipmaker tapping US pools at scale, and a possible template for peers seeking dollar-denominated capital and index inclusion. The post-pricing softness is the number to watch; a landmark deal that trades down invites caution on follow-on supply.

Elsewhere, General Fusion completed its de-SPAC with Spring Valley Acquisition Corp. III to become the first publicly listed fusion company, and a $4B Ohio data center financing sits on hold pending a tentative deal with possible Amazon involvement. KakaoPay Securities secured a dealer license to expand into IPO underwriting and bonds—incremental, but a new domestic sponsor entering the pipeline. B Capital separately noted India's deepening markets are stabilizing regional tech IPO conditions.

## What we're watching

Watch SK Hynix's Q2 print, which Korea Investment expects to miss consensus—the first real test of whether the export data translates to margin, and a read-through for the whole HBM complex.

Watch the Nextflex process and the reported Intel stake for confirmation: both are pending with unresolved terms, and either resolution would set a reference point—one for mid-cap component valuations, one for state involvement in chips.

Finally, watch concentration risk. TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix now exceed 30% of the MSCI Emerging Markets index; passive flows are amplifying every move in these names.

Bottom line: the export data says supercycle, but the tape says show me the earnings—and SK Hynix's Q2 will settle the argument.

# SK Hynix Crosses the Pacific: A $26.5B Nasdaq Listing Anchors the AI Memory Trade

The week's defining event was SK Hynix's $26.5 billion Nasdaq listing—the largest ever for a Korean semiconductor firm—arriving alongside macro data that validated the demand thesis behind it: Korean semiconductor exports surged 193% year-over-year in the first ten days of July, lifting total exports to a record $29.8 billion. Yet the equity market response was ambivalent. Shares fell 3.7–4.4% post-debut and slid another 6% intraday in Seoul, even as the U.S.-listed market cap surpassed Micron's. The dispersion between record fundamentals and cautious price action is this week's real story, and it should frame how corporate development teams read the AI supply chain from here.

## Deal activity

Dealmaking clustered squarely around AI infrastructure and semiconductor materials. The largest disclosed transaction was Element Solutions' agreed $14.5 billion acquisition of Solutics Advanced Materials, a specialty-materials play aimed directly at the chip supply chain. Anthropic's reported $19 billion arrangement with TeraWulf—structured with a $3.5 billion upfront component—drew scrutiny on financing adequacy, a reminder that compute-and-power commitments are outrunning conventional balance-sheet capacity.

The pattern beneath the headlines is vertical integration toward power and materials rather than horizontal consolidation. EQT is acquiring Copia Power, an integrated energy-and-digital-infrastructure platform; Nvidia and others are eyeing minority stakes in Lancium, the power backbone for Stargate; and a $4 billion Ohio data center project sits on hold pending hyperscaler commitment. Meta's reported $6.5 billion cloud-and-AI capacity push, target undisclosed, fits the same logic. On the materials side, Micron's $500 million minority investment in GlobalWafers is a supply-security move, not an acquisition. Domestically in Korea, the ~$580 million Nextflex sale stalled—final bids were delayed with no buyer named, a small but telling sign of cautious mid-market appetite. Government intervention also surfaced twice: a reported U.S. 10% stake in Intel and discussion of a 5% federal position in OpenAI.

## Capital markets

SK Hynix dominated the primary calendar. Beyond the $26.5 billion Nasdaq debut, the company filed a 424B4 prospectus supplement with the SEC, signaling a follow-on equity pricing event—confirmation it intends to keep tapping U.S. capital markets. The listing's mechanics rippled into FX: the ADR-driven dollar supply contributed to won weakness, with USD/KRW holding near 1,500.

Two smaller listings rounded out the week. General Fusion completed its de-SPAC combination with Spring Valley Acquisition Corp. III, becoming the first publicly listed fusion energy company on Nasdaq. Separately, KakaoPay Securities secured a dealer license, opening a path into IPO underwriting and bond origination. B Capital's read on India's deepening markets providing stable tech-IPO conditions is worth noting for regional exit planning.

## What we're watching

First, SK Hynix's pending follow-on: with the 424B4 on file, pricing terms and dilution impact will shape sentiment, especially against Korea Investment's flag that Q2 profit may miss consensus. The gap between record exports and a Q2 miss is the tension to resolve.

Second, the concentration risk. TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix now exceed 30% of the MSCI Emerging Markets index—a level that constrains incremental institutional buying and raises the stakes on any single-name disappointment.

Third, Samsung's foundry momentum: the reported tape-out of Tesla's AI5 chip on 2nm, plus Google Cloud's Gemini Enterprise rollout to Samsung's DX division, positions Samsung as both a logic supplier and an enterprise-AI reference customer worth tracking against OpenAI.

**Bottom line:** The AI memory trade is now a public-market franchise, but SK Hynix's soft debut warns that record fundamentals no longer guarantee a valuation premium.

# The Week the IPO Queue Became the Story

Three filings now sit in registration targeting a combined ~$3.5 trillion — SpaceX ($1.75T), Anthropic (~$965B), and OpenAI (~$852B) — an equity-supply test with no historical precedent. Against that backdrop, the week's announced M&A was modest in count but pointed in direction: chipmakers buying software.

## Deal activity

Qualcomm's ~$4B agreement for Modular is the week's signature transaction — the third case this cycle of silicon vendors acquiring the compiler/software layer rather than more silicon, aimed squarely at loosening the CUDA lock-in. Adobe's purchase of Topaz Labs (terms undisclosed) extends its pattern of buying model-adjacent imaging capability. Google/Wiz ($32B) remains in regulatory review with closing targeted for later this year.

## Capital markets

The convert market continues to reopen for AI infrastructure names: CoreWeave's 1.75% notes due 2031 traded above par this week, a meaningful signal after the winter selloff in neocloud credit. Quantinuum's June debut ($1.68B raised) continues to trade well, keeping the quantum window open. HBM supply reportedly sold out through 2027 shifts pricing power — and, eventually, capex financing needs — toward the memory complex.

## What we're watching

SpaceX float-size decisions, which will set allocation dynamics for the entire queue behind it; the Wiz closing timeline as the year's largest pending strategic deal; and TSMC's monthly sales print as the cleanest near-term read on AI accelerator demand.

Bottom line: supply of paper is about to test demand for the AI trade — and the acquirers who move before that repricing settle their currency questions first.

*(Example memo compiled from seed data as of Jul 6, 2026 — live memos begin with the first Friday run.)*
